After some investigations my conclusion is that the errors occours because the test use the same loop for client and for broker: the loop is closed while coroutine Broker.client_connected is waiting for tasks to close and thus a RuntimeError('Event loop is closed') is fired

the workaround that works for me is to add a sleep in the test function:

    def test_ping(self):
        @asyncio.coroutine
        def test_coro():
            try:
                broker = Broker(broker_config, plugin_namespace="hbmqtt.test.plugins")
                yield from broker.start()
                client = MQTTClient()
                ret = yield from client.connect('mqtt://localhost/')
                self.assertIsNotNone(client.session)
                yield from client.ping()
                yield from client.disconnect()
                yield from broker.shutdown()
                yield from asyncio.sleep(0.1)  ### <------ ADD THIS
                future.set_result(True)
            except Exception as ae:
                future.set_exception(ae)

        future = asyncio.Future(loop=self.loop)
        self.loop.run_until_complete(test_coro())
        
        if future.exception():
            raise future.exception()

This is an intentional limitation in Jira, not a bug in this library. Returning large numbers of results per page uses more memory, so allowing you to return, say, 1000 results per page is just setting you up for an OutOfMemory error in your instance.

On the Jira Server side, you can modify this hard upper limit by setting `jira.search.views.default.max` in **jira-config.properties**, but as mentioned above, you shouldn't do this without being very, very certain you have the memory overhead available.

On Jira Cloud, you can't increase maxResults to arbitrary values, as confirmed in [JRACLOUD-67570](https://jira.atlassian.com/browse/JRACLOUD-67570). Again, this is to protect against abuses. 

Suggest closing this issue as not a bug.

Hey Scott and Jonathan! I'm doing two things which are affected by the presence of missing rays. One is to apply the dual-PRF correction technique, which uses the local neighbourhood of the gates as reference for correction. @jfigui Added a few new comments.  Most of them are about setting `fill_value` and the `data` attribute of masked arrays.  

In general one should not depend on the `fill_value` of a masked array being correct as this value is not guaranteed to propagate.  

Additionally setting the `data` value for elements of a masked array which are masked in pointless.  NumPy assumes that these values can take on arbitrary values since the values are masked and does not attempt to preserve these values when operations are performed on the masked array.

Also should mention that it best to separate the addition of new functionality into different PR.  Each PR should only add one new feature or fix one particular bug.  This makes reviewing and merging easier.  Happy to get this one through as-is just something to keep in mind for the future.

```python
from gcloud import _helpers
from gcloud.bigtable._generated_v2 import bigtable_table_admin_pb2
from gcloud.bigtable._generated_v2 import table_pb2
from gcloud.bigtable.client import Client
from gcloud.environment_vars import TESTS_PROJECT


def main():
    # Set-up test env.
    _helpers.PROJECT = TESTS_PROJECT

    client = Client(admin=True)
    client.start()

    # New instance.
    instance_id = 'gcloud-test-issue-1524'
    instance = client.instance(instance_id, 'us-central1-c')
    instance.create()

    # New table object, but manually construct PB to create.
    table = instance.table('gcloud-python-test-table')

    # table_pb = table_pb2.Table(name=table.name)
    table_pb = table_pb2.Table()
    col_fam_pb = table_pb.column_families['col-fam-id1']
    col_fam_pb.gc_rule.max_num_versions = 1
    request_pb = bigtable_table_admin_pb2.CreateTableRequest(
        parent=instance.name,
        table_id=table.table_id,
        table=table_pb,
    )
    try:
        client._table_stub.CreateTable(request_pb, client.timeout_seconds)
    finally:
        instance.delete()
        client.stop()


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

We introduced `flow_control.max_lease_duration` (https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/pull/5020) to deal with bad actors - by default, if nothing happens to a message in 2 hours the subscriber will nack it.

troposphere is a python implementation of the CloudFormation templating language.  If you see it in the docs in CloudFormation, you should be able to do the same in troposphere.  Notice how you use 'HostedZoneName'?  If you look at the CloudFormation documentation for RecordSet, you'll see that option is listed there, and they behave exactly the same:

http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-properties-route53-recordset.html

You really shouldn't think of troposphere as a language of it's own.  It's just CloudFormation, but in python, rather than in JSON.  It has some extra helper features, but if you're looking for how to do something with a Resource Type or Property, your best bet is to consult the CloudFormation documentation for that ResourceType or Property.

Yeah, that sounds very interesting. That said, there's also potential for a
learning element where the name-ordering convention - if one exists - is
inferred at the level of the data source instead of at the individual
record.

For instance, after analyzing a sufficient sample, the peopleparser could
first figure out the defined source has a last-first-mi, first-mi-last or
some such ordering and then, if there's a clear conclusion, just apply that
to the entire set. This could speed up processing and reduce potential for
error on the inevitably iffy names (e.g. the Michael Jordans, etc.).

Maybe it's up to the user whether to activate source-level inferences and,
if so, how to parcel those out.

Hey - sorry this came as a surprise. It's been discussed in the [10/16 dev meeting](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1qZKFhNwEZwvNmMQIcrfPRDpGNlEZiPxsY3UOb2gf3bE/edit#heading=h.ph9o5jgd0qt6) and some recent [documentation meetings](https://docs.google.com/document/d/18-Pi78DD20CazDbu6heJUrI1ndwhCOYaHmDYnq-a_S4/edit#heading=h.p11kt6uw9kot).

At a high level, the motivations were:

1. Developers by default want to see docs for the `develop` branch, whereas end-users by default want to see the docs for the latest stable release.
2. We risk disconcerting typical end-users (coaches and beginner admins) by exposing them to overly-technical documentation unless they have explicitly requested it.

To your specific points:

> There's a number of greyzones where developers, advanced users and normal users overlap. These would require cross-referencing. ... Breaking up the docs means we have have to cross-reference via full URLs and not RST :ref: labels

I just audited, and there are no cross-references between user and developer docs. It should be easy to continue writing documentation that avoids this issue, but if we really need to it looks like [there are options](http://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/stable/ext/intersphinx.html) besides full URLs.

> Versioning becomes harder and un-aligned between developer docs and user docs -- you suggest not to build developer versions of user docs, but why not? They are for all the pre-releases and to verify how they look etc.

The most important thing is that _default_ documentation for end-users is the latest stable. It's fine building and showing beta and alpha docs for early review, especially since the community will likely help with beta/alpha testing. The main risk is exposing novice users to incomplete docs for functionality that hasn't yet been released, which would be confusing.

> Non-compatible with changes in previous versions of docs

Could you elaborate? I was wondering if there might be implications here but couldn't think of any specific consequences.
one disadvantage I hadn't thought of is that this will break the existing translations on crowdin. Consistency is good, but this may cause a fair amount of extra work for translators?

The `rewrite_update` method is there to narrow the update statement. If you only updated a single property on an object, it will only update that instead of the whole object: instead of `update t set x = ?` it will execute `update t set x['y']['z'] = ?` if possible.
(This has the advantage that we can resolve conflicts on the server side. For example if one client issued `set x['a'] = 10` and another `set x['b'] = 20` a conflict can be resolved because we know that different properties are updated. If it was issued like `set x = {"a": 10, "b": 10}` and `set x = {"a": 5, "b": 20}`, the second update would overwrite the `a` value.

Scout2 supports role (STS) credentials, but you need to instruct it to use these, it does not automatically identifies/guess roles that may be assumed. It works in a similar fashion than the AWS CLI:

http://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_use_switch-role-cli.html

If you ran Scout2 successfully with Account1, I'm assuming you configured your computer with credentials in Account1 and that the associated user is authorized to assume the role in Account2.

Then the only thing you'd need to do is add the following lines in your ~/.aws/config file:

[profile account2-readonly]
role_arn = arn:aws:iam::Account2AWSAccountID:role/Account2RoleName
source_profile = default

If you specified a profile name for your account1 user's credential, then use this value instead of "default".

I advise you test that you can assume the role with the AWS CLI first:

aws --profile account2-readonly iam list-users

If this works, then you can run Scout2 with the role's credentials:

./Scout2.py --profile account2-readonly

You'll have a new report-account2-readonly.html file ready to be viewed once the tool is done running.

> 1) this feels to me like something that should be in scripts/ rather than a command. 

Sure, I can change that later.

> (2) this feels very complicated to me. like, "I read the code multiple times and I'm still not 100% sure what it's doing or how to use it" complicated. as this PR stands, I don't think anyone would actually be able to use this functionality unless you showed them how. it's possible that all it needs is a tutorial to go with it, but it's also possible that it's more complicated than it needs to be.

In terms of usage, it's actually very simple. I believe the reason it seems complicated to use is that I tried to show complicated examples first that leverage jsonnet features. Let me try again by starting with simple motivating examples iteratively showing why and in what scenario particular feature would be required. I will get back with examples tomorrow...

> (3) I think this will mess up params that are specified by environment variables (that is, by std.extVar) -- when you do Params.from_file it will populate them based on the machine you're currently running on (or crash if they're not defined there), and then they're gone. If you were counting on them to be on another machine (say, if you run each experiment on some GPU machine), then they're gone. This feels like a problem.

If I understand you correctly, that shouldn't be a problem.  `generate-subexperiments` generates fully specified sub-experiment config files. The output experiment config files would not have such expressions `std.extVar("BIDIRECTIONAL")`- they would be plain jsons with everything specified based on generator config and root config. And if something like `std.extVar("BIDIRECTIONAL")` is actually used in generator_config_file / root experiment file it would work as expected.

> (4) I'm not a huge fan of ast.literal_eval. I don't really understand the part of the code that uses it, and I feel like that makes it even harder to understand.

Sure, I knew you would point `literal_eval` out : ) .. but I don't see any alternative. `execute` method takes in a source experiment config and executes a change on it which that Change-instance encodes. For simplicity consider Change as a `key` and `value` pair instead of `key_tuple` and `value_tuple`. `key` refers to key that you want to change: eg 'model.seq2vec_encoder.bidrectional', and value is the hard-coded or computed value that you want to set. The value can be any valid string: eg. "true", "false", ("[1, 2, 3]" if arrays were permitted!) or jsonnet expression "! true", "! false" or something that requires complicated referrence: "if current.something then true else false".

The following line: `value_evaluated = _jsonnet.evaluate_snippet("", define_current_var+value).strip()` returns a evaluation of jsonnet expression. Eg. `value = '{"hello": 1+2}'` would yeild `value_evaluated = '{"hello": 3}'`. We generally parse it by `json.loads` since we expect output of it to be dict. However, jsonnet expression can lead to any thing as we want: boolean, string, list etc hence `json.loads` is not appropriate.  Eg. `value = '!false'` would lead to `value_evaluated = 'true'`. `value_evaluated` is actually is string of python object which we want to set but it has json based constants since it came out of jsonnet. Hence we convert: "true"->"True", "null"->"None" etc and finally use `ast.literal_eval` to parse it as string/dict/list/boo etc. 

> (5) couldn't you just bake all of this into your jsonnet like .. and then just iterate over combinations of environment variables? wouldn't that accomplish the same thing in a much simpler way?

In terms of usage, IMO it would be much more cumbersome to manage, and it doesn't support many things that script would support.

1. Firstly, to use something like `std.extVar("BIDIRECTIONAL")` you would need to set and manage this environment variable explicitly. But for `currrent.model.seq2vec.bidirectional` you don't need anything. The referencing says that see that in current  state of experiment config, bidirectional is true or not and decide based on that. By adding `current.` variable to jsonnet you are saying that you can reference any part of the current experiment config arbitrarily with full flexibility.
2. Secondly, it's not just about iterating over combinations - the script gives you easily managable handle on giving names to the changes and organizing them in separate files. One can actually open these files, look at their names and check if things are correct. Changes identified by simplified names make things much more easy to manage.

3. It also gives you way to group the logically groupable changes so that they don't get combinated and one can identify them easily. .. I guess examples I give later would make this clear.

4. ..

It looks like this scenario that I brought up above, eg

```
a: (k1 = value1, k2 = value2)
b: (k1 = (value1, value2), k2 = (value3, value4))
```

can be written as 

```
k1, a: (value1, value2)
k2, b: ((value1, value2), (value3, value4)))
```

that is, using a special type of paired parameters, the `key - value type`. This I believe will make @pcarbo 's example:


```
  par: R(c(mu = 0,df = 2)),
       R(c(mu = 1,df = 2)),
       R(c(mu = 2,df = 4))
```

to output:

```
mu, df: (0,2), (1,2), (2,4)
@ALIAS: par = List()
```

So all we need is to change `dscrutils::dscreval()` behavior to generate these 2 lines and feed back to the DSC parser interface. This way we do not mess with query. The question is whether or not we really need this.

You don't have an engineered product if you don't pin versions.  What the customers / downstream users actually get is a random artifact created at build time.  It's development, sure, but not engineering.

I consider engineering to be a practice of following principled procedures to produce a product that consumers or other engineers can then consume.  It can vary within certain tolerances, but these must be known and documented.

Here, there are no set tolerances, no reproducible product being released.

If we apply release engineering we'd ideally get a hermetic build.  And this should apply for every commit.

@alex the problem with multi-AZ is that if connectivity drops between the AZs for any reason, you can easily end up with a split brain problem that can be very difficult to resolve.

That is obviously an extremely rare case, but etcd doesn't handle that situation very well.  A lot of thought about infrastructure needs to happen before you go multi-AZ, and it still probably should require 3 AZs/regions (with 1 acting as a simple arbiter even).

It's an extremely complex problem and I'm personally hesitant to recommend that situation because it grants a false sense of security.

Also, there's still the issue of backend data storage and which AZ that's in, how to route the DB requests to the appropriate DB, etc.

Multi-AZ is extremely complicated and I think that should be beyond the scope of this.

I think the behavior when you specify an absolute source path in the batch input without an explicit destination path is unexpected.

e.g.:

```
(.venv3) ~/Repos/globus-cli$ globus transfer async-transfer \
    ddb59aef-6d04-11e5-ba46-22000b92c6ec:/some-directory-on-ep1 \
    ddb59af0-6d04-11e5-ba46-22000b92c6ec:/some-directory-on-ep2 \
    --batch --dry-run
Enter transfers, line by line, as

    [--recursive] SOURCE_PATH [DEST_PATH]

Lines are split with shlex in POSIX mode: https://docs.python.org/library/shlex.html#parsing-rules
Terminate input with Ctrl+D or <EOF>

relative-123.txt
/absolute-456.txt
^D
{
  "DATA_TYPE": "transfer",
  "preserve_timestamp": false,
  "verify_checksum": true,
  "submission_id": "69bfe6d9-99fe-11e6-b0c1-22000b92c261",
  "destination_endpoint": "ddb59af0-6d04-11e5-ba46-22000b92c6ec",
  "source_endpoint": "ddb59aef-6d04-11e5-ba46-22000b92c6ec",
  "encrypt_data": false,
  "DATA": [
    {
      "destination_path": "/some-directory-on-ep2/relative-123.txt",
      "recursive": false,
      "source_path": "/some-directory-on-ep1/relative-123.txt",
      "DATA_TYPE": "transfer_item"
    },
    {
      "destination_path": "/absolute-456.txt",
      "recursive": false,
      "source_path": "/absolute-456.txt",
      "DATA_TYPE": "transfer_item"
    }
  ]
}
```

The first pair does what I would expect (`/some-directory-on-ep1/relative-123.txt` to `/some-directory-on-ep2/relative-123.txt`), but the pair from the absolute path doesn't (`/absolute-456.txt` to `/absolute-456.txt`). Given that I am omitting the batch destination path but I gave a command destination path, I would sort of expect `/absolute-456.txt` to `/some-directory-on-ep2/absolute-456.txt`.

I *think* that the behavior I would *expect* would be to take the last component off of the source absolute path (e.g. `absolute-456.txt` from `/absolute-456.txt`, `def/` from `/abc/def/`, `home/` from `/~/`) and then add it to the "command" destination path (e.g. `/some-directory-on-ep2/absolute-456.txt`, `/some-directory-on-ep2/def/`, `/some-directory-on-ep2/home/`).

instead of 

```
self.db.set(...)
self.db.persist()
```

On the other hand, we may also consider something simpler such as

```
self.db.set(foo, bar, True)
```

where the last parameter basically indicates an implicit `persist()` call for these cases that do not need journaling / batching.

I think we should take a look at the performance impact of this PR as it is then decide about further actions.

Fixed with #64 .

You need to update your dependency to either use `eth-abi` which is what this library was renamed to or pin to `ethereum-abi-utils<0.4.5`.  The python library `ethereum-abi-utils` will no longer receive any updates.  All future development is under the python library `eth-abi`

Also, you should upgrade to python3 **really** soon as support for python2 drops at the beginning of 2018.

Note that I chose to go with a restrictive input/output format to restrict the surface area that `eth-abi` needs to care about when encoding and decoding.  

I'm currently of the opinion that normalizing input values from things like dictionaries or objects should happen outside of `eth-abi`, and similarly, the decoder should return the values as a plain `tuple`.

My reasoning is that input validation for dictionaries and/or objects adds extra surface area that I'd rather push to higher orchestration layers.  It also would raise questions about whether dictionaries with extra keys should be allowed, etc.

The same goes with output types.  Simply returning a `tuple` of values is the simplest API and it fits well as a canonical representation for ABI tuple types.

I decided to find out what was going wrong in `compare_bytecode`. I compiled a simple smart contract against different versions of solidity. I noticed some differences in bytecodes for solidty version < 0.4.20 and >=0.4.20. Here are some results:
 ##### for solidity version 0.4.19
```python
In [6]: con['bin-runtime'] # expected_bytecode
Out[6]: '6060604052600080fd00a165627a7a72305820fe198797cf091375d4191836566e817de267342d7cfea15c50d3dc6ed958e91e0029'
# fast forward some steps of contract deployment
In [17]: w3.eth.getCode('0xbfBc3f7D8C0cF90E553Fc93ec95beE2d7104e7cd').hex() # deployed bytecode
Out[17]: '0x6060604052600080fd00a165627a7a72305820fe198797cf091375d4191836566e817de267342d7cfea15c50d3dc6ed958e91e0029'
```

 ##### for solidity version 0.4.20
```python
In [30]: con['bin-runtime']
Out[30]: '73000000000000000000000000000000000000000030146060604052600080fd00a165627a7a72305820917938062ffe211194022a5ffe160a7e99ba4e03a77b32685732efe661b16da90029'

In [36]: w3.eth.getCode('0x6dC65c405cF4053d9Adf018FD0eb7Ad52491bDb8').hex()
Out[36]: '0x736dc65c405cf4053d9adf018fd0eb7ad52491bdb830146060604052600080fd00a165627a7a72305820917938062ffe211194022a5ffe160a7e99ba4e03a77b32685732efe661b16da90029'
```
if you notice  carefully v0.4.20 has 46 extra hexadecimal digits compared to v0.4.19.
In fact, the first 46 digits in v0.4.20 have the  following format `r'73[0-9a-zA-Z]{40}3014'` and 
40 digits between 73 and 3014 happen to be the address at which they are deployed.

Also, the last 86 digits in both versions consist of the swarm hash. So, there's nothing wrong with the swarm hash.

PS: I've checked this regex: `r'73[0-9a-zA-Z]{40}3014'` for several contracts, but I couldn't find much info about it in the solidity docs. However, the release notes of 0.4.20 say that significant changes have been made to optimize generated  bytecode.

```python
from math import ceil
import collections
import math
import random
import time

def profile(to_wrap, runs=100000):
    start = time.time()

    for i in range(1, runs + 1):
        to_wrap(i)

    duration = time.time() - start

    return duration

def int_to_big_endian(value):
    byte_length = max(math.ceil(value.bit_length() / 8), 1)
    return value.to_bytes(byte_length, byteorder='big')

def or_int_to_big_endian(value):
    byte_length = math.ceil(value.bit_length() / 8) or 1
    return value.to_bytes(byte_length, byteorder='big')

def new_int_to_big_endian(value):
    return value.to_bytes(math.ceil(value.bit_length() / 8) or 1, byteorder='big')

def new_new_int_to_big_endian(value):
    return value.to_bytes(ceil(value.bit_length() / 8) or 1, byteorder='big')

def run():
    funcs = [
        int_to_big_endian,
        or_int_to_big_endian,
        new_int_to_big_endian,
        new_new_int_to_big_endian,
    ]
    results = collections.defaultdict(list)
    runs = 100000

    print('Performing test runs...')

    for i in range(1, 16):
        random.shuffle(funcs)
        for f in funcs:
            results[f].append(profile(f, runs=runs))

    for func, result_list in results.items():
```

The issue is `defaults` has added `blas` without `track_features` and `numpy`, etc. without `features`, but `conda-forge` has both `track_features` in `blas` and `features` in `blas` dependent packages. As the solver has for a long time preferred removing features, this is the behavior you are seeing. Though it is surprising that channel priority is not upheld here.

As a workaround, one can do `conda install conda-forge::blas=*=openblas` to pin the package to the `conda-forge` channel. Though if nothing your environment changes, this won't get tracked. ( https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/7355 ) So you may need to add a new package (e.g. `conda install conda-forge::blas=*=openblas six` when `six` is not present) or make some other non-trivial change to get `conda` to note the environment spec requirement with `blas`.

To explain this just a bit more:

PYTHONPATH is for putting your modules in a place so that python knows how to find them.  Some people use it to keep all their one-off scripts in one place, and import them.

PYTHONPATH causes problems when it includes packages that Anaconda or any other Python distribution you have installed on your system also includes.  Modules are found in PYTHONPATH first, before they get found in that distribution's site-packages folder.  You end up with the wrong version imported, and things break.

Many, many people (like you) put site-packages in PYTHONPATH to try to force a particular package to get picked up.  That generally breaks things in unpredictable ways.  It is best to, like Ilan says, unset PYTHONPATH, unless you are completely certain that you will be importing ONLY your code, and not any installed packages from anywhere.  It's safer to simply unset PYTHONPATH.

Each Python process creates its search path on startup.  You can see this with 

```import sys; print(sys.path)```  

If you have PYTHONPATH set, you'll see that its entries appear at the beginning of sys.path - this is why modules in the first folder clobber others.  If you don't have PYTHONPATH set, then nothing else gets in the way - only the site-packages folder local to the current python interpreter will get found.

This is completely separate from which python interpreter gets found.  PATH is the environment variable that determines that.  Conda's activate script takes care of setting PATH so that the desired interpreter in your env gets found.

I just reviewed @kalefranz's proposed channel priority integration test and I disagree with it. It was harder for me to see here with the much larger example, but _channel priority is working correctly_.

With `channel_priority: true`, the sort order for packages is:
1. channel priority
2. version
3. build number

With `channel_priority: false`, the sort order is:
1. version
2. channel priority
3. build number

In particular, I _deliberately_ chose not to use this sort order:
1. version
2. build number
3. channel priority

The reason for this is simple: _build numbers cannot be compared across channels._ There is no reason to assume that build 1 from channel A is newer than build 0 from channel B. In fact, the fact that this was occurring in conda 4.x was one of the _exact_ problems channel priority is meant to solve. Channels were playing "build number games" where they would purposefully bump build numbers to make their packages more likely to be selected.

I can’t accept the explanation offered in the linked issue without further evidence. Yes, conda favors fewer packages, but it favors higher versions and higher channel priorities more.

That said, I do have one idea that may help. Right now we do two “version” passes: one for the requested packages and one for everything else. I would suggest that more levels may be warranted; e.g.

1. Requested packages
2. Any other package that has ever been requested
3. The top level dependencies of any of the packages in 1 & 2
4. Any other dependencies

Layer 4 might even have multiple levels, a sort of breadth-first search in the dependency tree.

The point is that the lower level the dependency is, the less important it’s version maximization ought to be considered. Or put the other way, the higher up the tree we go, the more important its version should be.

This hang is because `python -m SimpleHTTPServer` handles only one connection at a time.

The client establishes a connection to GET the BAM file, and then establishes another connection to GET the index.  The blocked `read(4,` will return if the first connection finishes and SimpleHTTPServer gets to the pending request — but of course that never happens.

If one cared to support single-connection web servers, one could read the index before establishing the connection for the BAM file.  This would require a lot of reorganisation for samtools so I don't propose to fix this there.  Pysam could I suppose rearrange the file opening in _calignmentfile.pyx_'s `_open`, but I'm not sure I would recommend it!

So, at a bigger picture level, the reason that the `alert` commands use the relatively rare "tupled-parameter" style of adding multiple actions because they were just child properties on the entire alert resource. With action groups being a resource themselves, would it make more sense to just have the action group create command and then a specific command to add/remove actions (vice parameters on update)?

This would make it more like NSG rule creation where you simply run the command multiple times to add multiple rules. There's a downside to using the tupled-parameter approach in terms of tab completion and such, but it has the upside of being easily extensible with a command or parameter explosion.

The problem with this approach is that it makes downstream packaging harder as I can no longer just pull the latest stable version of each ```azure``` component from PyPI and package it for a distribution. I have to make sure I only pull the versions that are listed in the ```setup.py``` of the meta packages ```azure``` and ```azure-mgmt``` as otherwise these meta packages will have dependencies that cannot be fulfilled due to the use of the ```~=``` operator which introduces conflicts with higher major versions as the one specified.

Thus, I suggest either to not use the ```~=``` operator when you don't need it - since you mention you didn't introduce any incompatibilities since June 2016 - and just replace it with ```>=```. Or update the versions in the ```setup.py``` of ```azure``` and ```azure-mgmt``` to match the latest stable versions available on PyPi.

Another alternative would be to not mark any packages on PyPi as stable until the ```azure``` and ``azure-mgmt``` have updated the version numbers in their ```setup.py```.

The current situation is definitely not optimal.

The value of `sha` should *not* be `master` or `HEAD` - both will only match the latest version at install time, and will prevent updates subsequently.
On GitHub, the id of the most recent commit can be looked up easily (i.e. [commit/master](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/commit/master)).
Alternatively, setting `sha` to `HEAD` and running `pre-commit autoupdate` before anything else will effectively update the configuration file and therefore allow updates:

```
-   repo: git://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
    sha: master
    hooks:
    -   id: check-added-large-files
    -   id: check-byte-order-marker
    -   id: check-docstring-first
    -   id: check-case-conflict
    -   id: check-json
    -   id: check-merge-conflict
    -   id: check-symlinks
    -   id: check-yaml
    -   id: detect-aws-credentials
    -   id: detect-private-key
    -   id: end-of-file-fixer
    -   id: flake8
    -   id: pretty-format-json
    -   id: requirements-txt-fixer
    -   id: trailing-whitespace
-   repo: git://github.com/pre-commit/mirrors-pylint
    sha: master
    hooks:
    -   id: pylint
```

If you provide a reproduction case in a simple Bullet demo, I'll look if we can improve it.

Once you start setting the margin to tiny values (such as 1e-9) you are fighting the implementation. 1e-9 margins for various objects will disable robustness and reduce performance in GJK/EPA: it relies on some positive margin to avoid degenerate GJK simplex and to avoid EPA in shallow penetration cases. In many cases it is possible to use a larger margin and embed the margin inside the object:
btSphereShape and btCapsuleShape uses margin as radius
btBoxShape: the margin is already 'inside' the box, so the box is the right size, but slightly rounded corners.
btConvexHullShape: you can use some utility to create a smaller convex hull so that the shrunk convex hull + margin is the same size as the original btConvexHullShape.
If you apply above rules, then only the concave triangle meshes have tiny margins. Since we usually don't perform queries between two triangle meshes, each query will have sufficient margins.

Hello, I also encountered this issue in the current master branch. I believe that the problem originates from the use of `np.where()` in `_do_mstep()` to update only those entries in `self.startprob_` that are not zero. In `_do_mstep()`, if `self.startprob_prior` is the default value (1.0), and some positions in `stats['start']` contain zero entries, then the same positions in `self.startprob_` will also be set to contain zero entries, and hence will not be updated in future iterations of the EM algorithm. If after many iterations of the algorithm, all positions in `self.startprob_` contain zero entries, then the call to `normalize()` in `_do_mstep()` will result in the division of each entry in `self.startprob_` by the sum of the entries in `self.startprob_`, which is zero. This results in each entry in `self.startprob_` becoming `nan`. Removing the use of `np.where()` in `_do_mstep()` fixed the issue for me.

```python
model.startprob=start_probability
model.transmat=transition_probability
model.emissionprob=emission_probability
```

to 

```python
model.startprob_=start_probability
model.transmat_=transition_probability
model.emissionprob_=emission_probability
```

solves the issue.

That was just one example on how one possible timeout could be visualised. ;)

Just for reference here's how requests handles [basic](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/quickstart/#timeouts) and [advanced](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/#timeouts) timeouts.
In a nutshell, requests only knows two types of timeouts:
> The connect timeout is the number of seconds Requests will wait for your client to establish a connection to a remote machine (corresponding to the connect()) call on the socket. It's a good practice to set connect timeouts to slightly larger than a multiple of 3, which is the default TCP packet retransmission window.
> 
> Once your client has connected to the server and sent the HTTP request, the read timeout is the number of seconds the client will wait for the server to send a response. (Specifically, it's the number of seconds that the client will wait between bytes sent from the server. In 99.9% of cases, this is the time before the server sends the first byte).

So to summarize all options on timeouts I found up to now in this thread (excluding anything on *how* the timeout might actually be specified in code):

| # timeout                       | description                                                        | affected states                                                                |
| ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| 1. uber timeout                 | for total elapsed seconds, current (read) timeout                  | from request `start` to `end`                                                  |
| 2. pool queue timeout           | for waiting for an available connection from the pool              | from `queued_start` to `queued_end`                                            |
| 3. connection create timeout    | for waiting for connection establishment                           | from `create_start` to `create_end`                                            |
| 4. DNS resolution timeout       | probably only relevant on cache_miss, as a hit should take no time | from `resolve_start` to `resolve_end`                                          |
| 5. socket connect timeout       | for plain TCP connection establishment, requests' connect timeout  | for `sock_connect`                                                             |
| 6. connection acquiring timeout | current connection timeout, for whole connection acquiring process | from connection acquiring `begin` to `end`                                     |
| 7. new connection timeout       | for waiting for first response from a *new* connection             | from `sock_connect` to first byte received (i.e. `headers_received`)           |
| 8. HTTP header timeout          | for getting HTTP headers of response after request was sent        | from before `headers_sent` (or after last `chunk_sent`?) to `headers_received` |
| 9. read timeout                 | requests' read timeout for detecting slow server / connection      | after `headers_sent` and between each byte received from the server            |
| 10. response body timeout       | for limiting the time it takes to receive the response body        | from after `headers_received`, around all `chunk_received`, until `end`        |

Here are my comments:
- Do we really need timeout 6? It's just the sum of timeouts 2 and potentially 3. 
- Timeout 2 may be 0 seconds to never wait if the pool is empty and directly raise a specific exception.
- Timeout 10 might also be specified in relation to the number of bytes received, to abort downloads taking ages over a (suddenly) slow connection, but to still allow huge downloads over a normal connection.
- Some timeouts include others. We should think about the defaults in each individual case once the exact list of timeouts is settled.

I tried putting all the timeouts and states into a single diagram. Unfortunately, `blockdiag` doesn't allow a group / rectangle spanning multiple other groups, so I couldn't include all of them (which was the reason why I merged all diagrams in the first place). If you want to fiddle with it, you'll find the source code [here](https://gist.github.com/N-Coder/301485f598496aa307fa93dbc7bec490).
![aiohttp](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1306276/37425516-f3c23e22-27c3-11e8-8766-7f0e7ba70a01.png)

http://aiohttp.readthedocs.io/en/stable/client_reference.html#aiohttp.ClientResponse.json accepts `loads` optional parameter.

You could do call like `await resp.json(loads=partial(json.loads, parse_int=int_parser))`.

The only disadvantage is passing `loads` parameter everytime for getting json data.
It could be solved by adding `loads` param to `ClientSession` for setting up new default behavior, I'm open for Pull Request. 

We have `json_serialize` option already (while now I think the name is inconsistent with others, `dumps` would be better).

@SaiRaj07 Subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation, are you sure the resulting images are between -3 and 3 as specified by your bounds. Even if they are, it's probably not a good idea to do it like this, because you tell the attack to operate in the space of inputs with values between -3 and 3, but there is no well-defined way to get back from that space to the image space. In general, multiplying by std and adding the mean will get you out of the image space.

What you should do instead is to pass proper images (e.g. with pixel values in (0, 1) or (0, 255)) to the attack. The preprocessing (subtracting mean, dividing by std) should be part of your model or should be specified using the `preprocessing` parameter of the Foolbox model wrapper (`KerasModel` in your case). Please have a look at the example in the Readme.

I agree that some of the attacks can be unified like this, but I am not sure how well the Carlini & Wagner attack (and possibly other future attacks) will fit into this structure. CW basically performs an additional outer loop that optimizes a loss weighting hyper parameter. Extreme norms such as L0 and Linf also often require special treatment. But yes, unifying most of the gradient-based attacks would already be a great improvement and is absolutely doable.

If I understand your question, the shortest answer is yes: pretty much the only function that autograd provides is `grad`, which is meant to be able to differentiate as many Python functions as possible.

In the case of black box SVI, that may mean either that you're using the gradient of the variational family log density inside a Monte Carlo score function estimator, or that you're using the "reparameterization trick" to construct a Monte Carlo estimate of the ELBO itself and then computing that gradient. In either case, `grad` should be able to compute gradients for you.

The way Autograd is currently implemented, it executes the function being differentiated on a special `Node` object which behaves like a normal float (or array of floats) but records each element of computation as it goes along. The gradient is then calculated by working your way back through the computation (this is known as [reverse mode automatic differentiation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Automatic_differentiation#Reverse_accumulation)).

I was going to say yesterday that some kind of `dict` like functionality (which is [planned](https://github.com/numba/numba/issues/2096)) might also be necessary for storing the computation, but @mattjj and the other authors of the Autograd will know more.

You need to initialize the python logging system to avoid that error. You can add this code to do that:

```
import logging
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
```

But that just enables nsq to log an error; it needs a running tornado ioloop to work.

> I don't want to use http method

HTTP is the simplest and most flexible way to publish to nsq (and access a variety of APIs) from whatever application / context you have - there's an http client for every context. It's going to be way easier than forcing nsq.writer to work without a running tornado ioloop.

I am able to use nsq.writer without a *long term* tornado ioloop, but this is terrible and you should not do this:

```
import logging
import nsq
import tornado
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)

writer = nsq.Writer(['127.0.0.1:4150'])

@tornado.gen.coroutine
def do_pub():
    yield tornado.gen.sleep(1)
    writer.pub("test_topic", "hello world")
    yield tornado.gen.sleep(1)

tornado.ioloop.IOLoop.instance().run_sync(do_pub)
print("wrote one message to nsq")
tornado.ioloop.IOLoop.instance().run_sync(do_pub)
print("wrote another message to nsq")
```

use http

> I think a better solution is to batch samples based on the number of VCFs that can comfortably fit on a single node.

The main problem with that approach is that your variant calls will depend on how you structure your cohorts. I.e., if your pipeline gets split into 2 sub-batches instead of 4 sub-batches, you wouldn't expect to get the same variant calls at a sample level at the end, unless you had incredibly uniform batches. Additionally, if you have a batch of samples that you split into _n_ sub-batches, you won't wind up with a "squared off" variant matrix at the batch level, since the _n_ sub-batches will all (likely) have distinct sets of variants. Ideally, if we were going to split anything, we'd split by locus, which should be safe (with the "minor" issue of splitting up gVCF blocks if not working with BP resolution gVCFs).


Unfortunately, the server is very inconsistent about whether it returns a hash of information about a referenced entity or just an ID or list of IDs. This sometimes leads to some pretty epic hacking around. Check out `HostGroup.read`:

```
        Normalize the data received from the server.

        The server returns data in this format::

            {
                'id': 15,
                'name': 'name',
                'title': 'title',
                'subnet_id': null,
                'subnet_name': null,
                'operatingsystem_id': null,
                'operatingsystem_name': null,
                …
            }

        Normalize data to this format::

            {
                'id': 15,
                'name': 'name',
                'title': 'title',
                'subnet': {'id': …},
                'operatingsystem': {'id': …},
                …
            }
```

The current solution is to deal with these inconsistencies on a case-by-case basis. Here's a pretty typical example of how to do that:

```python
    def read(self, entity=None, attrs=None, ignore=()):
        """Compensate for the weird structure of returned data."""
        if attrs is None:
            attrs = self.read_json()
        # The server returns an ID for this attr instead of a dict of data.
        gpg_key_id = attrs.pop('gpg_key_id')
        if gpg_key_id is None:
            attrs['gpg_key'] = None
        else:
            attrs['gpg_key'] = {'id': gpg_key_id}
        return super(Repository, self).read(entity, attrs, ignore)
```

Writing boilerplate code like this all over the place sucks, of course. A nice enhancement would be to add logic in to the `EntityReadMixin.read` method that would deal with cases like this.

Anyway, what should be done? Declare a `OneToOneField`:

```python
'dns_id': entity_fields.OneToOneField(SmartProxy),
```

And then add in whatever logic is necessary to make `create()` and `read()` both work correctly. You can test the `read()` method by adding several lines to `ReadTestCase.test_attrs_arg_v1` and `ReadTestCase.test_entity_ids`. There are currently no unit tests for the `create()` methods.

This script shows two issues. First calling offset_for_times without a timeout or a -1 timeout, returns a copy of the TopicPartition objects you passed in. Second, calling `offset_for_times` without a timeout causes any subsequent `close()` on the consumer to hang.

```
import confluent_kafka
import uuid

topic_name = 'test-offsets'

conf = {
    'bootstrap.servers': 'localhost:9092',
    'api.version.request': True
}

# Create producer
p = confluent_kafka.Producer(**conf)

for i in range(1000):
    p.produce(topic_name, 'foo', timestamp=123456789000)
p.flush()

c_conf = {
    'bootstrap.servers': 'localhost:9092',
    'group.id': uuid.uuid1(),
    'session.timeout.ms': 6000,
    'api.version.request': True
}
c = confluent_kafka.Consumer(**c_conf)

topic_partitions_to_search = list(map(lambda p: confluent_kafka.TopicPartition(topic_name, p, 123456788000), range(0, 3)))
print("topic_partitions_to_search: %s" % topic_partitions_to_search)
# hack to make sure consumer has topic/broker metadata
c.subscribe([topic_name])
msg = c.poll()

offsets_with_timeout = c.offsets_for_times(topic_partitions_to_search, timeout=1.0)
offsets_with_neg_1_timeout = c.offsets_for_times(topic_partitions_to_search, timeout=-1)
offsets_without_timeout = c.offsets_for_times(topic_partitions_to_search)

print("offsets_with_timeout: %s" % offsets_with_timeout)
print("offsets_with_neg_1_timeout: %s" % offsets_with_neg_1_timeout)
print("offsets_without_timeout: %s" % offsets_without_timeout)

print("Right before close")
c.close()
print("Right after close")
```

I wouldn't say that support for `bytesarray` is particularly important here, but I am generally in favor of duck typing. I think that when we did the mypy typing for `typeshed`, we decided that the supported inputs would be `bytes`, `str` and "anything with a `.read()`".

Given that we're already calling `decode()`, I think probably replacing `bytes` with "anything with a `decode()` that returns either `str` or something with a `read()`" is reasonable. enough. I suspect the main problem with this is that it may cause problems if people provide `bytes` and `bytesarray`s encoded in something other than UTF-8, but that's already a problem (and one that's trivially solved by the fact that the first-class support is actually for `str` objects anyway), so I don't think it should be a blocker.

The most common practice is to make sure that the training and the test set are disjoint: no interactions should be shared between the two.

That way your evaluation gives you a better measure of the model's generalization performance: its accuracy on data it has not seen during training.

Yes, the complexity certainly *felt* high getting this all in. But looking back we're pretty much just piggy-backing on ``mopidy.js`` and ``mopidy.http.WebSocketHandler`` - so hopefully we won't have to maintain much beyond just keeping an eye on those.

The benefit really is the ability to move most of the application logic that is not strictly UI-related into Python, where it is easier to maintain, organise, and test in my opinion.

- Dealing with large tracklists is problematic on the client side, which feels like something that could probably benefit from being able to use generators.
- Although one could easily go overboard, having access to everything on PyPI could make it easier to implement things like caching and retrieving cover art and album info from other sources, etc. so we'll gain a lot of flexibility if we have direct access to all of the available packages via a Python model.
- There are some hard-coded configuration features that should ideally be moved out to ``mopidy.conf`` (e.g. https://github.com/pimusicbox/mopidy-musicbox-webclient/blob/develop/mopidy_musicbox_webclient/static/js/functionsvars.js#L77-L110). Doing this in Javascript is difficult because we don't have access to the mopidy configuration (as far a I know?). I also had it so that the entire configuration is stored in a cookie, but that seems wasteful.
- It might be useful to have access to Python's introspection features to figure out if a backend for example has searching implemented. The alternative is to have Mopidy implement a bunch of ``has_`` and ``can_`` methods so that we can determine which backends support what so that we can have the UI respond accordingly.

In general MVC feels easier to maintain over the long term, compared to having to keep track of the various JavaScript workarounds and global variables that Tornado injects in the html templates. But I could be wrong, and will defer to anyone that has experience with these things.

https://github.com/pimusicbox/mopidy-musicbox-webclient/commit/5437205e4df51eece55d5034bbe11a73799b5d10 optimises the jQuery selectors to use identifiers instead of attributes. Lists with roughly 2 000 tracks are now rendered about 80 times faster.

Also fixed a small issue with play icons not being updated in the ``#albumstable``, and replaced string references with their JavaScript constants.

The three stacks above pretty much tell everything:
During backtracking, the smt core first invokes the undo trail for the smt::context, then it calls the theory solvers to perform their undo operations.
The fpa2bv_converstion_trail::undo function assumes the reference to the attribute m_e is live, but in fact it isn't. A fix is to increment, and then in the undo function decrement, the reference counts on both the domain and range of the map maintained in the fpa2bv_conversion_trail class. So far, the trail object only has ownership of the range.

> I still don't understand why sometimes Git makes me do this merges.

When the upstream repo and yours have diverged, it tells you that they have diverged and suggests to do a `git pull` to merge in the remote changes. You need to remember what you did and either do a `git push --force` instead to overwrite the remote branch, or a `git pull --rebase` to replay your changes on top of the remote changes in case you want to include them. Both will give you a clean history without merges.

> The `peepholes` kwarg of the LSTM layer decides whether to include connections from the cell to the gates; [...] What I'd really like is something where if one of the arguments is passed as `None`, then the connection is not used, but this would make the code pretty ugly - we'd essentially have to define a new dot method which does nothing when one of the arguments is `None` or something.

I haven't taken a look at the code yet, but do you say allowing to set one of the constructor arguments to `None` would make the `peepholes` argument obsolete, plus make the class a lot more flexible? If so, I'd be in favor of not introducing the `peepholes` argument in the first place (we cannot easily remove it after the release). Setting a parameter to `None` is in line with how bias-less layers are implemented, and I think your solution of a `maybe_dot` helper method would be elegant enough.

> For example, it would make stacking the W_in_* matrices and using slice much more messy, at least at first glance.

Ah, I see... so it's more involved than adding a `maybe_dot` function.

> I was not fully aware of the full "history", but after reading issue #17 things are much clearer now. Sorry for the noise.

Wow, you're one of the few chosen people that know the full history of #17 then :) No need to be sorry, I think that's the longest thread in all of Lasagne and I wouldn't have expected anybody to read it. We might want to document the design choices you wondered about in our FAQ (#298).

> Maybe renaming the CustomRecurrentLayer to RecurrentLayer and RecurrentLayer to FullyConnectedRecurrentLayer would be more expressive though?

That might be a bit long, and `FullyConnectedRecurrentLayer` only tells part of the story -- note that `CustomRecurrentLayer` also allows to have in->hid or hid->hid be a stack of two or more fully connected layers. What about `RecurrentLayer` and `VanillaRecurrentLayer`? The latter would be the one that has single dense layers for in->hid and hid->hid and uses the stacked weight matrices trick to be faster. In any case, I'd use `RecurrentLayer` for the one that we expect to be used the most. I don't know which one that would be, I leave that up for the RNN experts here to decide.

> The main disadvantage here is that the convention across Lasagne is that the layers themselves take parameter initializers as keyword args

I wouldn't mind much about breaking with that convention and group parameters if that makes things easier. I didn't look at the code yet, though.

Reading up the RFC on %-encoding (https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc3986#section-2.1 to section 2.4) , it seems like it's up to the schema creator to determine what will be used as a delimiter to distinguish between data.  However, it does give this list as a guide

Must be encoded:
`gen-delims  =  ":"   "/"   "?"   "#"   "["   "]"  "@"`

permitted to be used un-encoded at the discretion of the interpreter:
`sub-delims  =  "!"   "$"   "&"   "'"   "("   ")"    "*"   "+"   ","   ";"   "="`

Un-encoded:
`unreserved  = ALPHA   DIGIT   "-"   "."   "_"   "~"`

If we can determine which of the sub-delims Connectwise doesn't use, they can be un-encoded.

I am not sure about the padding without looking into it more closely, but the built-in `bior4.4` wavelet is the same as CDF 9/7 aside from a different normalization of the coefficients.  (e.g.  dec_lo is scaled by sqrt(2) and dec_hi by 1/sqrt(2)).

Based on comparing where the 0's are in the `bior4.4` coefficients, the following slight modification to your cdf97 coefficients to have the same symmetry gives the expected exact reconstruction (to within numerical precision).  Note that two of the filters have a zero appended and two have it prepended.

```
factor=1
cdf97_an_lo = factor*np.array([0, 0.026748757411, -0.016864118443, -0.078223266529, 0.266864118443,\
                        0.602949018236, 0.266864118443, -0.078223266529,-0.016864118443, \
                        0.026748757411])
cdf97_an_hi = factor*np.array([0, 0.091271763114, -0.057543526229,-0.591271763114,1.11508705,\
                        -0.591271763114,-0.057543526229,0.091271763114,0, 0 ])
cdf97_syn_lo = factor*np.array([0, -0.091271763114,-0.057543526229,0.591271763114,1.11508705,\
                         0.591271763114 ,-0.057543526229,-0.091271763114,0, 0])
cdf97_syn_hi = factor*np.array([0, 0.026748757411,0.016864118443,-0.078223266529,-0.266864118443,\
                         0.602949018236,-0.266864118443,-0.078223266529,0.016864118443,\
                         0.026748757411])
cdf97 = pywt.Wavelet('cdf97', [cdf97_an_lo,cdf97_an_hi,cdf97_syn_lo,cdf97_syn_hi])
```

One point to discuss is whether `sort_enum_for()` should take the SQLALchemy base class or the Graphene SQLAlchemy Object Type as argument, or whether both should be allowed. Passing the Graphene has the advantage that the name of the enum type could be derived from the Graphene object type name which might be different from the SQLAlchemy base class name.
See this also: https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene-sqlalchemy/issues/29
@syrusakbary note that there's been quite a bit of improvements to these classes on #29, the latest versions are available as gists in that issue

Thanks for the report.  Is there any chance you can help us reproduce this, e.g. with a small example TF job that will show this behavior?  Or perhaps at least a screenshot of a page with some plots adjusted and some not?

Also, what version of TensorBoard are you using?

> It's sort of like a trap (if `repeatedField` is not updated, the graph will often still work, but it veils a bug)

Sure. And in the implementation here, if you don’t add more of these imperative conversions, the graph may often still work, but veils a bug. As you say in the description, _some graphs_ fail to load.

There is a disconnect between the output of the core parser and the desired format. You have to patch that somewhere, or you’ll have a bug. That much is clear. The only question is where to parse it, and doing it declaratively using the API that already exists for exactly this purpose seems much better to me.

> pbtxt format, not the proto ABI

As far as I know, pbtxt is isomorphic to the proto wire format. You can store your data either as binary or as pbtxt.[1] If pbtxt had an annotator for single vs. repeated field, we would have had the same problems changing `plugin_data` to be non-repeated: old pbtxt would no longer be valid, and we’d have to convert them anywhere that we parse pbtxt (which is currently nowhere, granted), etc.

[1]: And people certainly do store things as pbtxt—search Google for `inurl:pbtxt site:github.com` for examples.

First, I want to say thanks for contributing this! This obviously took a considerable amount of work and effort. I can tell that you put a lot of thought into these changes. 

I've reviewed all of the code and my comments are outlined above. Most (all?) of the issues are minor.

I was originally concerned what the impact on code size would be with a change on this scale, so I built the CC2538 target both with and without your changes, and then ran `size` for the `ot-ncp` example:

  `TEXT`| `DATA` | `BSS` | Total  | Total (Hex)    | Commit
--------|--------|-------|--------|----------------|-----------
 100736 |   2168 | 19488 | 122392 |        0x1de18 | 139e85f (Without Contexts)
 101016 |   2160 |  3148 | 106324 |        0x19f54 | e8eacd8 (With Contexts)

The `BSS` section has decreased in size dramatically, which is likely due to you putting `otContext` on the stack(See associated comment in source code). More importantly, it looks like there is not a significant increase in `TEXT` size (just 0.28%) due to this change, which is fantastic. 

It is my opinion that the changes reflected here are ultimately where OpenThread needs to be. The OpenThread API has been a bit short-sighted in this respect and I think these changes represent a marked improvement.

That being said, the forklift-nature of this API change presents a lot of practical challenges for us which I'm not immediately sure how best to resolve. The single biggest issue is at hand is that this change totally breaks all of our internal code which uses OpenThread. In other words, if this pull request is merged all of our stuff breaks. We would need to prepare things on our side first, and that takes engineering resources away from working on our internal milestones.

We need to discuss this more internally. More information to follow.

@NickNamerr, there seems to be an issue with your CoAP server running on the OTBR.  The CoAP client is rejecting the ACK because the ACK message's IPv6 source address does not match the request message's IPv6 destination address.

RFC 7252 Section 4.1 states:

> With no security, the endpoint is solely identified by an
   IP address and a UDP port number.

RFC 7252 Section 5.3.2 states:

> The source endpoint of the response MUST be the same as the
       destination endpoint of the original request.

Can you check your CoAP server implementation?

> As for Dtls::kPskMaxLength, this should be the same as OPENTHREAD_PSKD_MAX_SIZE. Note that RFC4279 does not apply here since we are using a new ciphersuite TLS_ECJPAKE_WITH_AES_128_CCM_8.

What I should've mentioned that I had in mind, is that if the DTLS was to be reused, e.g. by the user coap application with end-to-end encryption, then the kPskMaxLength should comply with the RFC4279 and be at least 64 bytes.

Hi, I am thinking about extending CoAP implementation on the client side to support retransmissions, non-piggybacked responses etc., and I'm wondering which way to go.

Passing the message back via callback seems to be one solution. In this case there will also be a need to revert the changes made by the lower layers. Maybe it would be needed to store the original size of reserved area in the message header so it could be restored after transmission?

Or, maybe a simple boolean information in the message header from upper layer to the lower one would be enough, whether it should take custody of the message or not. Another bool could inform the upper layer if the message is still being processed in the lower layer or not. But this approach would not allow the upper layer to use list pointers within the message.

Another one, less elegant and more memory consuming is to simply keep a copy of the original message in the CoAP client, and whenever a retransmission is required copy the contents to the another buffer. This way there is no need to pass the message back (CoAP does no seem to require this kind of information). Also the original message buffer could be used to store the retransmission-related data, so there would no need to keep it within the client itself.

What do you think?

@myoldusername well a quick and dirty solution is using a `fifo`

```
$ mkfifo in
$ tail -f in | ./fasttext predict-prob /root/ft_model.bin - 2
```

and in another shell you do:

```
 $ echo "hello classify me" > in
```

so you will get

```
$ tail -f in | ./fasttext predict-prob /root/ft_model.bin - 2
__label__spam 1 __label__ham 1.95313e-08
```

A better approach will be to get the output redirection in your client command line like this:

```
$ nohup ./fasttext predict-prob /root/ft_model.bin - 2 < in &
```

and then something like

```
$ echo "hello classify me" >> in
```

but this could be a bit tricky to be handled. I strongly suggest a wrapper like my node client or a python btw.

> How about a flag to certbot renew that simply output text like renewed, unchanged without other decoration text?

While slightly different, what if we ensured certain strings are output in both of these cases in normal output, documented this, and set up tests to prevent regressions? Sure, `grep` or whatever tool you use has to parse a little more data and the strings have to be specific enough to avoid false positives, but this should get the functionality you want without introducing more flags to Certbot.

> And another somewhat related question: do you think the nginx plugin can be adapted to this multi-container situation?

Probably not. The nginx plugin needs to be able to run the `nginx` command line tool to have nginx reload the config after making changes. It also uses this to get the version of nginx being used. Getting this to work across containers seems hard.

Perhaps in the future we could adapt the nginx plugin to work without this, but it would require the user to restart nginx themselves (sometimes during Certbot execution if you're having the nginx plugin perform the domain validation challenge) and Certbot would be unable to know if nginx was able to reload using the config Certbot modified. Currently, if nginx fails to reload after Certbot makes a change, it automatically rolls back your config and this behavior would be lost as well if Certbot can't run the `nginx` command.

> What do you see as the future way of changing and updating records? sf.Contacts.update() seems pretty straightforward.

It's straightforward when you only consider the force.com API, but starts to get a little unclear when you consider adding support for other services (https://help.salesforce.com/HTViewHelpDoc?id=integrate_what_is_api.htm).

What I'm proposing is simply something like this:

```
sf.force.Contacts.update()
sf.chatter.do_something()
sf.tooling.do_something()
sf.apex.do_something()
sf.bulk.do_something()
```

It's much more representative of the backend services that they're implementing and allows for modularity between services. It would also allow for special per-service handling independently of other service implementations. In a nutshell, it's the separation of concerns I'm after with this change. `Salesforce` should manage connectivity and any common aspects (error handling, serialization and deserialization, etc), but the actual service implementation can be (and *should* be) independent of one another.

Here's my override class, by the way, with a test. If you like, I can pull request this in as a default.

```python
import simple_salesforce
from decimal import Decimal
from collections import OrderedDict
import responses

class DecimalPrecisionSalesforce(simple_salesforce.Salesforce):
    """A shim over the library Salesforce client providing Decimal numeric values instead of float"""

    def query(self, query, **kwargs):
        url = self.base_url + 'query/'
        params = {'q': query}
        # `requests` will correctly encode the query string passed as `params`
        result = self._call_salesforce('GET', url, params=params, **kwargs)

        if result.status_code != 200:
            simple_salesforce.api._exception_handler(result)

        return result.json(object_pairs_hook=OrderedDict, parse_float=Decimal)

    def query_more(self, next_records_identifier, identifier_is_url=False, **kwargs):
        if identifier_is_url:
            # Don't use `self.base_url` here because the full URI is provided
            url = (u'https://{instance}{next_record_url}'
                   .format(instance=self.sf_instance,
                           next_record_url=next_records_identifier))
        else:
            url = self.base_url + 'query/{next_record_id}'
            url = url.format(next_record_id=next_records_identifier)
        result = self._call_salesforce('GET', url, **kwargs)

        if result.status_code != 200:
            simple_salesforce.api._exception_handler(result)

        return result.json(object_pairs_hook=OrderedDict, parse_float=Decimal)


def test_fixed_precision_responses():
    response_with_float = """{"floaty_value": 0.3}"""

    # Passing in instance/token/session_id to avoid constructor making a net call to authenticate
    client = DecimalPrecisionSalesforce(instance="instance", security_token="token", session_id="session")
    with responses.RequestsMock() as rsps:
        rsps.add(
            responses.GET,
            url=client.base_url + 'query/',
            body=response_with_float,
            status=200,
            content_type='application/json'
        )
        response = client.query('foo')
    assert response['floaty_value'] == Decimal('0.3')

```

@andreif, great work, I've left a couple very small comments.

Here's the state of our error reporting:

1. Failures in `Check` are (supposed to be) reported to the CLI by the step generator. The step generator may return errors which today may or may not have already been logged. As we can see here in this bug, the plan executor doesn't log the errors coming out of the step generator, so they can be dropped.
2. Failures in step applications are logged by post-step events. This hasn't been changed recently, so I would be surprised if there were issues here.
3. Errors coming from step execution other than the application of the steps themselves (e.g. pre and post-step callbacks returned errors, we registered the same URN twice) are logged by the step executor.

The de facto contract that the step generator has with the plan executor is that it has already done all error logging that needs to be done before returning an error. We should strive to centralize our error reporting logic in one place, either in the step generator or in the plan executor. In practice this would mean that the step generator always logs every single error that it sees, or that the plan executor logs every error that comes out of step generation.

I really like Martin's idea to have a few major "modes". Given those modes we can set all the other more complex parameters accordingly -- so for example our Save mode would default to high amounts of retries and long timeout values because we don't care about real time streaming so much as accuracy of the bitstream.

On Sep 28, 2016, at 4:53 AM, Martin Thurau <notifications@github.com<mailto:notifications@github.com>> wrote:


I also like the piping feature, but for a different reason: I watch a lot of Twitch and for certain things (like bigger Dota2 tournaments) I have the problem that they broadcast on US and SEA timezones. So I sometimes use livestreamer to simply dump the stream to a file and tell VLC to open that file. This way I get an easy timeshifting setup where I can pause my playback to things like housework and then come back later to resume where I left off (and get a free "skip boring things" feature).

Besides this, I have the feeling that this comment track goes in a strange direction: From reworking the CLI to a pretty meta discussion about the general direction (hint, hint: Maybe that should be discussed elsewhere ;) )

To come back to the actual discussion: I also think that the CLI is complicated, sometimes confusing and repetitive. I have the feeling that we could do pretty well with splitting it up into different subcommands like git and similar tools do. This way we could provide a quick way to reach different "modes". I.e:

  *   streamlink play <url>: current default behaviour
  *   streamlink save <url>: dump to a file
  *   streamlink proxy <url>: launch a http server that serves the stream
  *   streamlink info <url> --json: show information about the stream a JSON

This way we could factor our all common arguments (i.e. outgoing proxy settings, location of config file, this kind of stuff) and have the specific options on the subcommand. I'm not sure how to deal with plugin specific options, thou.

Ohhh, actually, I bet this is a side-effect of #341 – you have some task that's blocked waiting for an fd to become readable, and that fd got closed. Then cancel the waiting task, so trio tries to tell the kernel that it doesn't need to know about when the fd becomes readable after all, and the kernel is like "wtf are you talking about, there is no such fd" (because it was already closed).

Does that sound like it matches your script where you hit this issue?

If so, then the long-term solution is to fix #341, and the short-term workaround would be to make sure not to close a stream/socket/fd while there is still a task waiting on it. One way to accomplish this would be to make sure to cancel the task first before closing the stream, rather than afterwards.

Here's another case where `accept_nowait` would be useful: with unix domain sockets, it's possible to do no-downtime upgrades by binding the new listening socket to a temporary name, then atomically renaming it over the old listening socket, and then having the old process drain and handle any pending connection attempts.

Draining a listening socket means, handle all currently pending attempts when you know that no new ones will be arriving. That's a pretty natural fit to `accept_nowait`, and not really doable with blocking `accept` alone.

> I think it would be best to focus on comparing Trio to asyncio with "pure" async/await. Another aspect of the comparison is whether the differences are due to fundamental aspects of asyncio or simply bugs or missing functionality (which can in theory be fixed).

s/trio/curio/ and you have *exactly* described what that blog post is about, especially the "is this fixable" part :-). My conclusion is that when you layer async/await on top of callbacks and protocols then the abstraction leaks a lot and this isn't really avoidable.

And AFAIK the planned asyncio changes don't really affect much -- the new thread-local loop tracking is a great feature that solves [one of the problems I noted](https://vorpus.org/blog/some-thoughts-on-asynchronous-api-design-in-a-post-asyncawait-world/#getting-in-touch-with-your-event-loop), but it's really the least essential problem. `asyncio.run` isn't a replacement for `curio.run`/`trio.run`, because the key feature of the latter is that they have a general strategy for detecting when your whole program has finished, whereas in `asyncio` you still have to manually keep track of the lifetime of all connections to figure out when it's safe to shut down the loop. (And `asyncio.run_until_complete` doesn't really solve this -- it waits for *that* coroutine to finish, but if there are Tasks still running then they just get unceremoniously abandoned. That can't happen in curio/trio.)

(Heck, in asyncio if you're using the high-level `async/`await`-oriented functions like `open_connection` and `start_server`, and you have a protocol where you speak last, like an HTTP server, then it's actually *impossible* to deterministically do a graceful shutdown that doesn't risk truncating connections, because there's no way to know when asyncio has finished draining your connection's send buffer and its safe to exit. This particular bug is fixable, but it's an example of how tricky asyncio makes lifetime management that there's this fundamental a bug in the core code. This class of bug can't happen in curio/trio.)

Anyway, I'm definitely not saying that that blog post is the best possible documentation, and I appreciate the suggestions. This is a huge area, different people find different aspects compelling, ... improving the docs/marketing/communication-in-general is an ongoing process :-). So this kind of feedback is very much appreciated -- I'm just trying to give some quick hints as to what the proper documentation might eventually say so that you don't have to wait that long :-).

I originally made this maximally paranoid about the minor behavioral differences, but on reflection I'm not sure that level of paranoia is necessary -- I think it would reasonable to make `@async_generator` produce native generators on CPython 3.6+ without a deprecation period, and continue to permit `@async_generator(allow_native=False)` to use the old pure-Python version always. That reduces the API footprint (exposing `@async_generator_legacy` and `@async_generator_native` feels like a wart to me). Thoughts?

The general idea when adding a new output language is to perform step-by-step translation of the compiler's internal representation (IR) into the target language. The internal representation is a list of [basic blocks](https://github.com/kensho-technologies/graphql-compiler/blob/master/graphql_compiler/compiler/blocks.py), some of which contain [expressions](https://github.com/kensho-technologies/graphql-compiler/blob/master/graphql_compiler/compiler/expressions.py). The translation happens with a [language-specific "lowering" pass](https://github.com/kensho-technologies/graphql-compiler/blob/master/graphql_compiler/compiler/ir_lowering_gremlin.py) that aims to translate the IR into equivalent IR that more closely resembles the target language. After the lowering step is complete, there's a one-to-one mapping between the lowered IR and the target output language, so it's very simple to [emit the resulting query code](https://github.com/kensho-technologies/graphql-compiler/blob/master/graphql_compiler/compiler/emit_gremlin.py).

There is more than one pytz module, one in your jep interpreter and one coming over with pandas from the shared main interpreter. If you add pytz to the list of shared modules then it should work. I don't see anything else we can do. 

You mention there may be fundamental underlying issue with shared modules and I agree. Shared modules was implemented as a last resort work-around for extension modules that won't support sub-interpreters. Shared modules will never be an elegant solution, and it is troublesome that once you start using them it seems to suck in more and more modules but I can't think of any way to stop it.

Sub-interpreters are a documented part of the C-extension API for python and other projects such as mod_wsgi try to use sub-interpreters but they run into problems with native extensions, similar to what we see on jep without shared module. In my opinion, the real underlying issue is with the python ecosystem, module writers will not write their extensions to support sub-interpreters, this makes it very difficult to actually use sub-interpreters, which means they aren't used much, and since sub-interpreters aren't used much module writers have no reason to support them.

The only elegant solution I can come up with would be for all python extension writers to support using their modules in sub-interpreters. I don't know what issues caused pandas to be shared, it may be a bug in pandas or it may have been one of their dependencies. I would recommend you try to figure out why pandas cannot be used safely in sub-interpreters and write issues with the projects that are causing problems asking them to support sub-interpreters.

IMO subgraph should be just a normal graph, and it's better to not make it to be different with the top level graph. Extracting blobs from subgraph to parent level makes the global graph looks messy, and as mentioned above it can potentionally hit name conflict. Re. support in c2, I feel it's lacking proper support of nested graph in general, a lot of places don't descend into subgraphs to do the same processing as for the top-level graph, so I don't want to use it  to judge whether a feature should be supported in onnx or not.
@dzhulgakov @tjingrant What is the solution we can start to implement ?

OptimizedRNN still needs it as e.g. LSTM has optional initial states and optional sequence lengths - both are optional independently.

The current specification is very simple, i.e. positional arguments to operators. I don't think it depends much on how implementations handle it.

As for "sequence" type - it's fundamentally different. Here we're talking about passing several other tensors from the graph together as an op, not representing a single value of type list. Equivalent would be having Pack operator which would take (still variadic) number of input arguments and produce sequence as an output. But it introduces additional indirection in the graph and makes it harder.

The reason I don't like input_arg_count is that it changes the default behavior in unexpected way. If I have optional Inputs 2 - X and Input 3 - Y with input_arg_count I don't know any more whether op.input[2] represents X or Y. Since it's not a common path, it's more probably that people would misuse it. Whereas with empty strings it's kind of obvious that the input is missing if op.input[2] is empty. And if implementation doesn't handle it properly, it'd fail in quite predictable way by tripping over an empty string.

Hmmm, I personally believe, once we add this field, backend should/will use and check it to ensure that it's reading tensor with right order. In this way, the idea of introducing an attribute for each op is better and clearer (in this case, backend will use and check the attribute). @dzhulgakov @ezyang @ebarsoum may share thoughts please.

@botev I respectfully disagree on the fact that we want to go down to the very mathematical description of programs. ONNX is not intended to be a general purpose math expression library and is specifically focused on neural networks, so finding a proper abstraction that does not make optimization unnecessarily hard is going to be preferred. Of course, the exact balance is something needing detailed discussion.

As an example, Dropout can be implemented as

mask = Rand(m, n)
Y = X * mask * scale

but in a neural network sense this probably isn't the best way for us to represent the neural network semantics.
